class_schedule.topic: Corporate Strategy and the Multi-Business Firm
learning_objectives: Define corporate strategy and the corporate advantage concept
Explain the role of the corporate center in multi-business firms
Evaluate whether diversification creates or destroys value

lecture_notes.introduction: Corporate strategy addresses the fundamental question: does owning multiple businesses create more value than each business would create independently? If not, the corporation should be broken up.
key_concepts: Corporate advantage: the value created by the corporate center
Diversification premium vs. conglomerate discount
Corporate parenting: how the center adds value to business units
Portfolio management vs. synergy management
The 'better-off test': are businesses more valuable together than apart?
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing whether General Electric's diversification created or destroyed value over the past 20 years, using sum-of-the-parts analysis
setup_requirements: Textbook: Collis & Montgomery, Corporate Strategy (3rd ed.)

review.value: The corporate center must add value that exceeds its cost; businesses must be 'better off' in the corporation than as standalone entities
Market values most diversified firms at less than the sum of their parts; evidence that diversification often destroys value

learning_objectives: Evaluate M&A strategies and their success rates
Identify sources of value in acquisitions vs. common pitfalls
Analyze due diligence and integration best practices
readings: Christensen et al., The Big Idea: The New M&A Playbook (HBR)

lecture_notes.introduction: M&A is the most dramatic corporate strategy action, yet studies consistently show that 60-80% of acquisitions fail to create value for the acquirer. Understanding why is critical.
key_concepts: Value sources: synergies (cost and revenue), market power, capabilities acquisition
Winner's curse and hubris hypothesis
Due diligence: financial, operational, cultural, legal
Integration approaches: absorption, preservation, symbiosis
Post-merger integration: the make-or-break phase
lecture_notes.demonstration: Post-mortem analysis of a failed acquisition (AOL-Time Warner) and a successful one (Disney-Pixar), identifying the key differences

review.value: Acquiring companies often overpay based on projected synergies that are harder to realize than expected; the premium should not exceed achievable synergy value
Cultural clash is the #1 reason mergers fail; successful integrators invest heavily in understanding and bridging cultural differences

learning_objectives: Apply transaction cost economics to vertical integration decisions
Evaluate the benefits and risks of vertical integration

lecture_notes.introduction: Vertical integration is the decision to produce internally rather than buy from the market. Transaction cost economics provides the framework for making this boundary decision.
key_concepts: Transaction cost economics: bounded rationality, opportunism, asset specificity
Benefits of integration: coordination, quality control, IP protection
Risks of integration: bureaucratic costs, reduced flexibility, capital intensity
Taper integration: partial ownership of supply chain
Platform strategies and vertical disintegration
lecture_notes.demonstration: Make-or-buy analysis for a tech company: should Apple continue making its own chips or outsource?

review.value: When an investment is tailored to a specific transaction partner, market transactions become risky; vertical integration protects against hold-up problems
Producing some of a component internally while also buying from external suppliers; maintains market knowledge and bargaining power while retaining integration benefits

class_schedule.topic: Corporate Portfolio Management and Resource Allocation
learning_objectives: Apply portfolio frameworks to allocate corporate resources
Evaluate business unit performance using financial and strategic metrics
Make divestiture and investment decisions at the corporate level
readings: BCG and GE-McKinsey Matrix Application Guide

lecture_notes.introduction: The corporate center's most powerful tool is resource allocation: deciding which businesses get capital, talent, and management attention. Getting this right separates great corporations from mediocre ones.
key_concepts: BCG matrix: growth-share classification for resource allocation
GE-McKinsey matrix: industry attractiveness vs. competitive strength
ROIC trees: decomposing returns to identify value drivers
Divestiture criteria: when to sell, spin off, or shut down
Real options: viewing investments as options on future opportunities
lecture_notes.demonstration: Portfolio analysis for a diversified corporation: classifying business units, identifying resource allocation priorities, and recommending divestitures

review.value: Decomposes return on invested capital into operating margin and capital efficiency, then further into revenue growth, cost structure, and asset utilization
Sell when: the business is worth more to another owner, the corporate center cannot add value, or resources would generate higher returns elsewhere

class_schedule.topic: Global Corporate Strategy and Organizational Design
learning_objectives: Design organizational structures for multi-business corporations
Manage corporate centers: lean vs. involved parenting styles
Align corporate governance with strategy execution

lecture_notes.introduction: The organizational structure of a corporation must support its strategy. Chandler's famous observation that 'structure follows strategy' remains a central principle of corporate management.
key_concepts: Chandler's thesis: strategy determines structure
M-form (multidivisional) structure and its variants
Matrix structures for global corporations
Corporate center roles: portfolio manager, synergy manager, parental developer
Corporate governance: board structure, executive compensation, shareholder activism
lecture_notes.demonstration: Organizational redesign exercise: recommending the optimal corporate structure for a company transitioning from domestic to global operations

review.value: Multidivisional structure: autonomous divisions managed by a corporate center that handles strategy and resource allocation; enables related diversification
Strategic planning (involved center), Strategic control (balanced), Financial control (hands-off, return targets only)

class_schedule.topic: Dynamic Corporate Strategy: Transformation and Renewal
learning_objectives: Evaluate corporate venturing and strategic innovation
Design strategies for corporate renewal and reinvention

lecture_notes.introduction: In a rapidly changing environment, corporations must continuously evolve. The challenge is exploiting current businesses while exploring new ones, a tension known as organizational ambidexterity.
key_concepts: Ambidexterity: balancing exploitation and exploration
Corporate venturing: internal ventures, CVC, incubators, accelerators
Strategic renewal: when and how to transform the corporate portfolio
Disruption theory: why incumbents struggle with disruptive innovation
Platform and ecosystem strategies for corporate renewal
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case analysis of Microsoft's transformation under Nadella: from Windows-centric to cloud-first, analyzing how corporate strategy, culture, and structure all changed

review.value: Structurally separating exploration units from exploitation units while integrating them at the senior leadership level for strategic coherence
Creating or investing in new ventures to access innovation outside the core business; CVC investments provide strategic windows into emerging technologies

questions.question: A pharmaceutical company wants to acquire a biotech startup for $2 billion. The startup's standalone value is estimated at $1.2 billion. Identify the conditions under which this acquisition would still create value for the acquirer's shareholders.
questions.answer: The acquisition creates value if the synergies exceed the premium. Premium = $2B - $1.2B = $0.8B. The acquirer needs at least $800M in present value of synergies to justify the price. Potential synergies: (1) Revenue synergies from the acquirer's sales force and distribution network accelerating the biotech's drug commercialization. (2) Cost synergies from eliminating duplicated R&D infrastructure and G&A. (3) Risk reduction from the acquirer's financial strength funding costly Phase III trials. (4) Pipeline synergies from combining research capabilities. The acquirer should discount projected synergies by 30-50% to account for integration risk and execution challenges.

rubric.key: Notes execution risk and synergy discount

questions.question: Using transaction cost economics, explain why Netflix chose to produce original content (vertical integration into production) rather than continuing to license content from studios.
questions.answer: Transaction cost economics predicts integration when: (1) Asset specificity is high: Netflix needs content tailored to its platform and data-driven audience insights; generic licensed content becomes less differentiating. (2) Frequency of transactions is high: Netflix needs a continuous pipeline of content, making repeated market transactions costly. (3) Uncertainty is high: studios began withholding content for their own streaming services (Disney+, HBO Max), creating supply uncertainty. (4) Hold-up risk: content owners could raise licensing fees once Netflix was dependent on their libraries. By integrating into production, Netflix secures its content supply, differentiates its offering with exclusives, and captures the full value of its audience data for content development.

questions.question: A conglomerate's stock trades at a 25% discount to the sum-of-the-parts value of its business units. Explain what this means and propose three actions the corporate center could take to close the discount.
questions.answer: A 25% conglomerate discount means the market believes the corporate center is destroying value: the businesses would be worth 25% more if operated independently. Actions to close the discount: (1) Divest unrelated businesses that lack synergy with the core portfolio, returning proceeds to shareholders. (2) Reduce corporate overhead by streamlining the center and demonstrating clear value-add for retained businesses. (3) Increase transparency by providing detailed segment reporting so investors can properly value each business. Additional actions: improve capital allocation discipline (stop subsidizing weak businesses with strong ones), consider a breakup or spin-off if the discount persists.

rubric.key: Proposals address root causes of discount

questions.question: Explain why a corporation pursuing a strategy of related diversification through synergy should use a different organizational structure than one pursuing unrelated diversification as a financial holding company.
questions.answer: Related diversification requires intensive coordination between business units to realize synergies (shared resources, knowledge transfer, cross-selling). This requires: shared service functions, cross-business unit teams, matrix elements, and an involved corporate center that manages interdependencies. A cooperative M-form or matrix structure is appropriate. Unrelated diversification operates each business as an independent profit center with minimal cross-business coordination. This requires a competitive M-form structure with a lean corporate center focused on financial targets, capital allocation, and executive talent management. Applying a synergy-focused structure to unrelated businesses creates unnecessary bureaucracy; applying a hands-off financial structure to related businesses leaves synergies unrealized.

major_assignments.title: Corporate Strategy Assessment and Recommendations
major_assignments.description: Select a publicly traded multi-business corporation and conduct a comprehensive corporate strategy assessment including portfolio analysis, synergy evaluation, M&A history review, and organizational design analysis. Deliver strategic recommendations for value creation.
objectives: Analyze a corporation's portfolio using established frameworks
Evaluate whether the corporate center creates or destroys value
Assess M&A history and integration effectiveness
Recommend corporate strategy actions to increase shareholder value
deliverables: Portfolio analysis using BCG and GE-McKinsey frameworks

criteria: Accurate classification of all business units
Data-driven assessment of industry attractiveness and competitive position
Synergies between businesses quantified where possible
Honest assessment of premium or discount
Multiple options evaluated with pros and cons
Financial data accurately sourced and presented
Rigorous portfolio and value creation analysis
Honest assessment of corporate center value-add

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of corporate strategy concepts

case_discussions.description: Quality of case preparation and contributions to in-class case discussions

major_assignments.description: Corporate strategy assessment and recommendations report

midterm_examination.description: Covers diversification, M&A, and vertical integration

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including portfolio management, organizational design, and transformation

course_policies.attendance: Mandatory for case discussions. Cases must be prepared before class.
course_policies.late_submissions: Corporate strategy report penalized 10% per day.
course_policies.collaboration: Report is individual work. Case discussion preparation may involve study groups.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All financial data and analysis must be original or properly attributed.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to financial databases for corporate analysis. Presentation software.
